paragraphs.text: Again “God tested Abraham, and said to him: …Take thy son… and go into the land of Moriah, and offer him there… for a burnt offering upon one of the mountains which I shall tell thee of” (Gen 22:1–2). So that it should not be said that Abraham was in a frenzy, the fulfillment is delayed three days. “And Abraham rose up… in the morning… and clave the wood… and took with him two young men and Isaac… and went” (Gen 22:3). But to Sarah he did not reveal why he was going, because it was not commanded to reveal it — for she certainly would also have resolved to go and to take part in the offering, as she had taken part in the promise of Isaac’s birth. Abraham did not reveal it also that the household might not hinder him and produce strife in his tent, that the people of the land might not gather and take away the boy, or compel him to put off the offering for several days. If Abraham feared to tell even those two young men whom he took with him to the mountain, much more must he have feared the many. And he who out of fear did not wish to reveal his purpose to the two youths, when they had already ascended the mountain and Isaac asked about the sacrifice, prophesies to him, as he had prophesied to the servants left below at the mountain.

“And having bound Isaac… he laid him on the altar… and… drew the knife” (Gen 22:9–10), but the angel of the Lord held back his hand. And lest Abraham should think that for some defect his sacrifice was rejected, the angel said to him: behold, thou hast shown thy reverence before God, and through the one most dear to thee thy love for the Lord of all is revealed. So Abraham became glorious both because in his heart he had already slain his son, though he did not slay him in deed, and because he believed that his son would even after death rise again and return with him; for he did not doubt the truth of what was said to him: “For in Isaac shall thy seed be called” (Gen 21:12).

“And Abraham saw a ram caught in a thicket, and took the ram and offered him up for a burnt offering in the stead of his son” (Gen 22:13). That the ram had not been there earlier is shown by Isaac’s question; and that the thicket was not there either is shown by the wood placed on Isaac’s shoulders. The mountain gave forth the wood, and the wood the ram, that the ram caught on the tree, and afterward slain in the son’s stead, might prefigure the day of the One who, like a lamb, was nailed to the tree and tasted death for the whole world. “And he called… to him a second time out of heaven… By Myself have I sworn, saith the Lord… that in blessing I will bless thee, and in multiplying I will multiply thy seed… and in thy Seed — that is, in Christ — shall all the nations of the earth be blessed” (Gen 22:15–18).
